policy_text:
Starting September 1, 2026, we are moving to a hybrid collaboration model for our San Francisco and New York offices. The default expectation is two days per week in-office (Tuesday and Wednesday), with the third day flexible at the team level.

Why we're doing this: Customer-facing teams have asked for more in-person time for cross-functional planning, and our engineering retros consistently rate hybrid sprints highest. We piloted this model with the Sales team in Q1 and saw measurable lift in pipeline velocity.

What's changing for you:
- Two anchor days (Tue/Wed) for cross-team collaboration
- One flexible team-choice day, owned by your manager and team
- Caregiving, medical, and accessibility exceptions are guaranteed, not discretionary — apply through People Ops, not your manager
- Fully-remote roles remain fully-remote; this policy applies to those already assigned to an office
- Three-month ramp: optional in June–August, expected starting September

We'll run a pulse survey 30 and 90 days after rollout and adjust based on what we learn. This is a starting point, not a final answer.

parsed_policy.summary: San Francisco and New York offices moving to mandatory two-day in-office hybrid model with one flexible team day starting September 2026.

actions.content: Hey, need to flag something early — the Sacramento commute is a 4-hour round trip by train. I get the rationale for in-office days, but at this distance I'd need to talk through what's realistic for me before September.

I think the hybrid setup is going to help us — pipeline reviews are more effective face-to-face, and having the team together two days will help with rep onboarding and retention. The flexibility on exceptions shows leadership actually thought this through.

We need to talk about how this hybrid policy applies to my role. I was recruited as remote-first from Portland with three kids—relocation isn't possible and weekly flights to SF would be untenable with my caregiving responsibilities.

Quick heads up — the two-day requirement is going to clash hard with my daycare pickup at 5:30. I can make one day work consistently, but two means I'm either paying $50/day for backup care or missing pickup. Can we talk through what 'guaranteed exceptions' actually looks like fo

Need to flag something before September — the two-day mandate doesn't align with my custody schedule or my client coverage model. My accounts are in Stamford and I've got alternating weeks with the kids; can we talk through how the exception process actually works?

I relocated to Boulder three years ago under the remote-first policy and my partner just started a tenure-track position at CU—moving isn't an option for us. I need to understand how the guaranteed exceptions process works and confirm I'll qualify given my family situation.

Hey, need to flag something before September. With my spouse on night shifts and the kids' schedule, I can't reliably do two fixed days plus a team day—my commute from Walnut Creek is 75 minutes each way and childcare pickup is non-negotiable at 5:30. Can we talk through what the

Quick heads up — I'm 100% supportive of collaboration days, but the 3pm pickup window is non-negotiable for me with two elementary kids. Can we lock in Mon/Fri as my in-office days so I'm not scrambling mid-week?

I need clarity on how the remote exception process works — I relocated to Raleigh two years ago for my partner's tenure-track job and was hired explicitly as permanent remote. Moving back to SF isn't an option for us.

I need to discuss an exception for the hybrid policy — I relocated to Portland after going remote, and my child's immunocompromised status makes both the commute disruption and open office environment a serious health risk. Can we talk through options?

Quick question — I was hired explicitly as remote from Austin because of my SE travel load. Does the guaranteed exception process cover roles that were remote from day one, or am I looking at relocation?

The two-day hybrid model makes sense from both a fiscal and culture standpoint — we're leveraging our SF lease commitment while keeping flexibility for talent retention. Happy to walk through the real estate math with anyone who wants to see the numbers.

Following up on my message from day 0 — I want to formally request a full-remote exception given the Sacramento situation. Happy to come in for critical team events or offsites, but a regular commute schedule isn't sustainable at 4 hours round-trip.

Following up on our conversation — I need formal confirmation that remote roles recruited as remote-first are exempt. Relocating or flying 10+ hours weekly with three kids isn't viable, and I took a pay cut specifically for this flexibility.
Honestly, the commute thing is going to be a problem. Frustrated.
Honestly, the commute thing is going to be a problem. Frustrated.

I need to formally request an exception under the caregiving provisions. The 5:30pm daycare pickup with late fees makes the two in-office days unworkable without backup care that would cost me $400+ per week.
Hey, need to talk through the hybrid policy — I was hired explicitly as remote from Austin and just signed a new lease here. Is the guaranteed exception process something we can walk through?

Following up on my earlier note — I need a formal exception for the custody weeks. My clients are in Stamford and I've built these relationships over six years without needing to be in the NYC office. Can we lock in remote flexibility for alternating weeks?

Following up on my earlier message — I relocated to Raleigh with my family two years ago based on the permanent remote offer, and we bought our first home here. Can you confirm my exception will be approved given those circumstances?
Honestly, the commute thing is going to be a problem. Frustrated.

I submitted an exception request last week but haven't heard back — with my child's medical schedule and the health risks of an open office, I need to know if remote work will remain an option for my situation.

Hey, need to discuss the hybrid policy — I was hired remote from Seattle and just signed a year lease here. Moving to SF/NYC isn't financially viable for me right now, so I'm hoping the guaranteed exceptions apply to my situation?

I was hired as fully remote three years ago specifically because I'm in Portland with two kids and school schedules. Even with the exception process, I need to understand how this applies to roles that were never office-based to begin with.
Putting this here for visibility — the commute impact wasn't considered.

I need to talk through the exception process — I relocated to Denver when I was hired remote-first, and flying in twice a week isn't viable with my daughter's childcare setup. What are my actual options here?
Putting this here for visibility — the commute impact wasn't considered.
Honestly, the commute thing is going to be a problem. Frustrated.
Quick one — with my Hoboken commute and daycare pickup, the two-day mandate means 13-hour days when my partner's traveling. Can we talk about how the guaranteed exceptions work for my situation?
Quick question — I was hired explicitly as remote from Denver, no SF/NY office within reasonable distance. How does the hybrid requirement apply to people in my situation?

I need a definitive answer on my exception request — my family's entire life is in Boulder now, and my partner's tenure track position isn't movable. What's the timeline for HR to confirm remote status?

I need to flag something before this lands — I'm already seeing burnout signals across the team, and I'm worried the two mandatory days will push our remote folks over the edge. Can we talk through how exceptions will actually work for my team's situation?

Following up on my earlier message — I need formal confirmation that my remote status from Austin will be grandfathered in, since I was hired explicitly as remote and I'm already traveling 40% for customer engagements. Can you escalate this to HR so I have documentation before Se

It's been three weeks since I submitted my exception request and I still don't have an answer. My child's medical team is here in Portland and we can't just uproot that — I need to know if I'm going to have a job in September or if I should start looking now.

I was hired as fully remote from Chicago and I've been planning a move to Minneapolis to be near family. With the hybrid mandate, I need to understand if my role can stay remote or if I need to rethink this entire plan — the exceptions language helps, but I need certainty before

Following up on my message earlier this month — I need formal confirmation that my remote arrangement will be honored. My childcare setup was built around the terms I was hired under, and I can't relocate or change that structure.

It's day 28 and I still don't have written confirmation of my remote exception. I need that in writing before the policy goes live, or I'm going to have to make some hard decisions about my future here.

themes.label: Loss of flexibility
themes.description: Employees read the policy as reducing autonomy and flexibility they rely on to do good work.

themes.label: Caregiving burden
themes.description: Employees are worried the policy does not account for caregiving, medical, or accessibility constraints.

themes.label: Commute strain
themes.description: Employees expect the change to create meaningful time and energy costs outside work.

quotes.text: Hey, need to flag something early — the Sacramento commute is a 4-hour round trip by train. I get the rationale for in-office days, but at this distance I'd need to talk through what's realistic for me before September.

Following up on my message from day 0 — I want to formally request a full-remote exception given the Sacramento situation. Happy to come in for critical team events or offsites, but a regular commute schedule isn't sustainable at 4 hours round-trip.

recommendation.title: Clarify rationale, ownership, and exception paths before rollout
recommendation.rationale: The strongest theme is Loss of flexibility. This recommendation reduces ambiguity, moves sensitive exceptions out of ad hoc manager discretion, and gives employees a visible feedback loop before the policy becomes permanent.
recommendation.suggested_rewrite:
Before this policy takes effect, we will publish the business rationale, who is affected, what is required versus optional, and the exact process for exceptions or accommodations. Managers will receive consistent guidance, and People Ops will own sensitive exception decisions so employees do not have to negotiate personal circumstances with their direct manager.

We will treat the first 30 days as a feedback window, review cohort-level pulse results, and adjust the policy where the impact is higher than intended.

summary: Predicted -10 eNPS over 30 days. 3 LinkedIn updates expected. Top concern: Loss of flexibility.
